ontology.foodon_gap_note: FoodOn has 'soy beverage' (FOODON_03301103) as the closest match; no dedicated 'less sugar soy milk' term exists. Matched at beverage-level category.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 code A0EJW covers soya milk generically; no specific reduced-sugar variant code exists. Used as closest defensible anchor.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No specific SNOMED CT code identified for less-sugar soy milk beverage. SNOMED CT anchor omitted to preserve ontology honesty.

serving.natural_language_portion: A typical serving of soy milk (less sugar) in Singapore is one cup of approximately 250 ml, commonly consumed at breakfast or as a mid-morning beverage.

nutrient_source.title: Soy Milk (Less Sugar) — Singapore HPB Foodo Nutrient Composition Database
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Nutrient values sourced directly from HPB Singapore Foodo database entry for Soy Milk (Less Sugar), cr_id D02030005, with confidence 0.95. All core macros and secondary nutrients are exact matches from this primary regional source.

gi_source.title: Glycemic Index of Soy Milk — University of Sydney GI Database (Atkinson et al., 2008; Foster-Powell et al., 2002)
gi_source.source_match_notes: GI value of 34 for soy milk is drawn from the University of Sydney GI Database, which reports GI values of 30–44 for soy milk products. The less-sugar variant is expected to have a GI at the lower end of this range given reduced added sugar content. GL per serving calculated as: (GI × carbohydrate_g per serving) / 100 = (34 × 11.1g) / 100 = 3.8. Carbohydrate per 250g serving = 4.45 × 2.5 = 11.1g.

hypertension.key_factor: Added sugar present; low sodium (13 mg/100g) is favorable but sweetened plant milk with added sugar and low fibre (<1g/100g) is not recommended for hypertension management.
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should switch to genuinely unsweetened soy milk, as the added sugar in this less-sugar variant contributes to metabolic load and does not confer the cardiovascular benefit of unsweetened soy protein; sodium content is low at 13 mg/100g and is not the primary concern.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Added sugar present (4.35g/100g); classified as a sugar-sweetened beverage (SSB) under diabetes dietary guidelines despite the 'less sugar' label.
type2_diabetes.guidance: Persons with Type 2 Diabetes should avoid this product and choose genuinely unsweetened soy milk (sugar_g = 0), as even reduced-sugar sweetened soy beverages raise postprandial glucose and qualify as SSBs under clinical dietary guidelines for T2DM management.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Soy protein has established LDL-lowering properties, but added sugar in this variant partially offsets cardiovascular benefit; fat content is low (1.8g/100g) with negligible saturated fat.
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: For cardiovascular disease management, unsweetened soy milk is preferred over this less-sugar variant, as soy protein (2.65g/100g) supports LDL reduction but the added sugar content (4.35g/100g) introduces metabolic risk that attenuates the net cardiovascular benefit.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Added sugar (4.35g/100g) with minimal fibre (0.7g/100g) may promote low-grade inflammation; soy isoflavones have anti-inflammatory properties but are insufficient to offset added sugar at this dilution.
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Individuals with rheumatoid arthritis should choose unsweetened soy milk to benefit from soy isoflavones without the pro-inflammatory effect of added sugar present in this less-sugar variant.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Soy Milk (Less Sugar) is a processed plant-based beverage made from soaked and ground soya beans blended with water and a reduced quantity of added sugar. It is widely consumed in Singapore and across Southeast Asia as a breakfast drink or mid-morning refreshment, commonly available in hawker centres, coffee shops, and supermarkets. The beverage has a mild, slightly sweet, beany flavour with a thin, watery texture compared to dairy milk. Per 100g, it provides 44 kcal, 2.65g protein, 1.8g fat, 4.45g carbohydrate (of which 4.35g is sugar), 0.7g dietary fibre, and 13mg sodium. Calcium content is low at 10mg/100g unless fortified. The glycemic index is approximately 34 (low GI), but the presence of added sugar classifies this product as a sugar-sweetened beverage (SSB) under clinical dietary guidelines. Despite the 'less sugar' designation, this product is not suitable for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes or those managing hypertension through dietary sugar restriction. Unsweetened soy milk is the clinically preferred alternative for metabolic health. Soy protein in this beverage contributes to LDL cholesterol reduction, but the added sugar partially offsets cardiovascular benefit. The product is vegan, dairy-free, gluten-free, and halal-suitable. It is a source of plant-based protein and soy isoflavones, though at the dilution typical of commercial soy milk beverages, isoflavone concentrations are modest.

confidence_reason: Nutrient values sourced directly from the HPB Singapore Foodo database (cr_id D02030005) with a reported confidence of 0.95. This is the primary canonical regional source for Singapore food composition data. GI value is drawn from the University of Sydney GI Database for soy milk, which is an approved GI source. All mandatory multilingual names are present for a Singaporean food item.
notes: monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, trans_fat_g, potassium_mg, vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, and water_g are set to -1: detailed fatty acid profile, micronutrient panel, and moisture data for this specific less-sugar soy milk product are not reported in the HPB Singapore Foodo database entry (D02030005) as of 2026-04-16. saturated_fat_g of 0.3g is estimated from USDA FoodData Central soy milk reference (NDB 16222) as the HPB source does not report saturated fat separately; this is documented as an adapted value. cholesterol_mg is 0.0 as soy milk is a plant-based beverage and contains no cholesterol (naturally zero by plant origin). The 'keto' dietary tag present in the input has been removed as soy milk with 4.45g carbohydrate per 100g does not meet standard ketogenic dietary thresholds. GI value of 34 is from the University of Sydney GI Database for soy milk; the less-sugar variant is expected to fall within the reported range of 30–44 for soy milk products. GL per 250g serving = 3.8.
